HomePage.craftTitle: Design, code, and delivery should read as one system.
HomePage.craftDescription: I start with product shape, keep interfaces calm, and use motion only when it sharpens hierarchy.
HomePage.craftStatement: Readable at a glance. Precise in motion. Ready for production.
HomePage.finalTitle: Need a frontend engineer who can shape both the surface and the system?
HomePage.finalDescription: See the case studies, then reach out for product work, collaboration, or hiring conversations.

productThinking.title: Product-first thinking
productThinking.description: Start from user flow and decision value, then choose the engineering depth that supports it.

modernFrontend.title: Modern frontend systems
modernFrontend.description: Use React 19, App Router patterns, and transition-aware UI without turning the surface into a demo.

deliveryFocus.title: Delivery without noise
deliveryFocus.description: Keep the codebase predictable, the UI restrained, and the path from prototype to production short.

TechStackPage.title: Tech Stack

categoryDescriptions.languages: Core programming languages used in product delivery and frontend systems.
categoryDescriptions.frameworksLibraries: Framework and state patterns for scalable interfaces.
categoryDescriptions.database: Data layer technologies used for product persistence and analytics.
categoryDescriptions.styling: Design-system and styling approaches for maintainable UI.
categoryDescriptions.testing: Quality and confidence tooling for stable releases.
categoryDescriptions.tools: Execution environment, collaboration tooling, and AI-agent surfaces used daily.
categoryDescriptions.backend: Server and platform technologies used in full-stack delivery.

TechStackPage.methodDescription: I optimize for predictable delivery: clear architecture, reusable design systems, measurable performance, and collaboration-friendly code.

showcase.title: From newbie code to expert systems
showcase.description: Selected stacks where I can show how my implementation changes when the bar moves from “it works” to “it scales.”

react.summary: Replace manual loading state with React 19's built-in action model.
react.improvement: Moves from ad-hoc useState flags to useActionState — the form's action prop drives the async lifecycle, isPending replaces manual toggles, and success callbacks compose cleanly.

nextjs.summary: Use PageProps and async params to keep the route server-first with full type safety.
nextjs.improvement: Moves from a client-heavy page shell to an async Server Component typed with PageProps<'/route'> — the globally available helper generated by next typegen that resolves params as a Promise and eliminates manual type annotations.

typescript.summary: Use discriminated unions and satisfies to encode domain rules in the type layer.
typescript.improvement: Moves from a loose object literal to a discriminated union (kind: 'code' | 'narrative') verified with satisfies — TypeScript catches missing fields at the definition site, not at the call site.

playwright.summary: Structure tests as readable steps, not a flat sequence of awaits.
playwright.improvement: Moves from a single navigation to named test.step blocks — each assertion is scoped, failures point to the broken step, and the test report reads like a product spec.

supabase.summary: BaaS speed with full Postgres control — auth, real-time, and RLS in one platform.
supabase.improvement: Moves authorization out of application code and into Row Level Security policies at the database layer, reducing surface area for bugs and making access rules auditable alongside the schema.
supabase.detail: I reach for Supabase when I need to prototype at BaaS speed without giving up Postgres depth. Row Level Security encodes authorization rules inside the database itself — keeping API routes thin and access policies version-controlled. The real-time layer replaces polling with a WebSocket push channel, a natural fit for collaborative or live-updating surfaces. Supabase's auto-generated REST and typed client mean the schema becomes the contract, and breaking changes surface at build time rather than in production.

agentEngineering.title: I use agents like working surfaces, not magic shortcuts.
agentEngineering.description: The strongest signal is not that I can prompt Codex or Claude Code. It is that I can shape bounded tasks, explicit contracts, verification loops, and proof artifacts so the work is engineerable by both humans and agents.

taskDecomposition.title: Task decomposition & spec writing
taskDecomposition.claim: I turn vague requests into implementation-ready work packets with clear scope, constraints, and success criteria.
taskDecomposition.workflow: I start from repository truth, then break the job into bounded tasks, interfaces, and acceptance checks that can survive handoff.
taskDecomposition.codexRole: Codex is my primary implementation surface when the repo needs grounded edits, test runs, and iterative verification.
taskDecomposition.claudeRole: Claude Code is useful when I want a second execution lens on spec shape, prompt clarity, or task framing before or alongside coding.
taskDecomposition.contract: Every task gets a brief with the goal, hard constraints, changed surfaces, expected artifact, and the exact proof needed to call it done.
taskDecomposition.verification: I do not stop at 'the agent answered.' The output must line up with source, docs, and tests.

toolFluency.title: Tool / orchestration fluency
toolFluency.claim: I know how to route work across CLI tools, repo instructions, prompts, and browser automation without losing the thread.
toolFluency.workflow: I keep the execution surface explicit: AGENTS.md, task-specific prompts, repo paths, and the command surface the agent is allowed to use.
toolFluency.codexRole: Codex handles repo-native implementation, shell-driven inspection, and test-backed iteration inside the actual project context.
toolFluency.claudeRole: Claude Code complements that with alternate reasoning on prompt shape, decomposition, and high-context review loops when I want contrast.
toolFluency.contract: The agent does not get a vibe-based ask. It gets the exact file or subsystem scope, allowed tools, and the expected output format.
toolFluency.verification: The orchestration is only good if another engineer can rerun it, inspect the same artifacts, and reach the same conclusion.

verificationHarness.title: Verification & harness design
verificationHarness.claim: I build verification into the workflow so agent output becomes evidence-backed engineering instead of plausible text.
verificationHarness.workflow: I close the loop with repo-native checks, page-level assertions, and markdown decision records that explain what the system proves.
verificationHarness.codexRole: Codex is where I wire the source edits, route metadata, and executable checks into a single working harness.
verificationHarness.claudeRole: Claude Code is useful as a contrasting reviewer for whether the brief, prompt surface, and observed result still line up.
verificationHarness.contract: The contract ends with runnable checks and named proof artifacts, not with a narrative summary alone.
verificationHarness.verification: For this portfolio, the proof is the combination of UI, source data, tests, and markdown documentation all agreeing.

harness.title: The workflow I expect an agent task to survive
harness.description: If a workflow cannot survive a handoff, a rerun, and a verification pass, I do not consider it production-ready engineering.

brief.title: Brief and constraints
brief.description: Start with the actual goal, hard boundaries, changed surfaces, and the acceptance criteria that matter.

surface.title: Agent execution surface
surface.description: Give the agent the repo contract, task prompt, and relevant paths so it can operate inside a bounded system.

verification.title: Verification loop
verification.description: Run the repo-native checks that prove the output works, not just that the patch exists.

proof.title: Proof artifacts
proof.description: Leave behind docs, source, and test evidence that another engineer can inspect without re-discovering intent.

AboutPage.title: About Me
AboutPage.intro: I'm a front-end developer focused on clear product interfaces, maintainable frontend systems, and recent AI-agent workflows that still hold up under engineering scrutiny.
AboutPage.heroStatement: Calm product interfaces, clear frontend systems, and motion that earns its place.
AboutPage.portraitCaption: I work best where product judgment, visual restraint, and implementation quality need to hold together under real delivery pressure.
AboutPage.aboutMeDescription: I'm a front-end developer with 3+ years of experience in building web applications using modern technologies like React. I have a strong understanding of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, and I'm always eager to learn new frameworks and libraries. My development philosophy centers around clean, maintainable code and a user-first approach. I believe that great design is not just about aesthetics but also about usability and accessibility. I strive to create products that are both beautiful and easy to use for everyone.

2026.title: Portfolio rebuild and narrative-driven architecture
2026.description: Redesigned this portfolio around story-first sections, tokenized theming, stronger international UX consistency, and explicit AI-agent engineering proof.

2025.title: Product-focused frontend delivery
2025.description: Built and shipped multiple product interfaces with improved filtering, modal flows, and maintainable component boundaries.

2024.title: Foundation in modern React ecosystem
2024.description: Expanded expertise in React, Next.js, and TypeScript with a strong emphasis on accessibility and performance.

product.title: Solve product problems first
product.description: Start from user and business goals, then select the simplest implementation that can scale.

craft.title: Design and code as one system
craft.description: Build reusable UI primitives and design tokens so consistency improves as features grow.

team.title: Communicate with clarity
team.description: Prefer explicit naming, predictable structures, and documentation-ready prompts/checklists so both teammates and agents can execute safely.
